frames.caption: Dump every value into a HashSet for O(1) membership (it also dedupes). longest starts at 0.
frames.note: Sorting then scanning would cost O(n log n). Hashing does better: with O(1) average membership we can ask "is x−1 / x+1 present?" instantly, which is exactly what lets us find run boundaries without sorting. We trade O(n) extra space for O(n) time.

frames.caption: x=100: is 99 in the set? No → 100 is a run START. Begin counting (length = 1).
frames.note: x is a run START because x−1 is NOT in the set — nothing precedes it, so this is the smallest element of its run. This guard is the whole trick: a run is only ever walked from its smallest member, so across the entire algorithm each element is visited by exactly one inner walk → O(n) total, not O(n²).

frames.caption: 101 is not in the set → this run ends. Its length is 1.
frames.note: The while test failed, so we have walked the run to its top. The run we just measured is the maximal block of consecutive integers containing this start.

frames.caption: longest = max(longest, 1) = 1.
frames.note: Record this run's length against the best seen so far. longest is the running answer; only a run longer than everything before it moves the needle.

frames.caption: x=4: is 3 in the set? Yes → 4 is mid-run, skip it.
frames.note: x−1 IS in the set, so x sits in the MIDDLE of some run and will be counted when we reach that run's smallest element. Skipping here is what prevents re-walking the same run from every member — dropping this guard is the classic mistake that makes the algorithm O(n²).

frames.caption: x=200: is 199 in the set? No → 200 is a run START. Begin counting (length = 1).
frames.note: x is a run START because x−1 is NOT in the set — nothing precedes it, so this is the smallest element of its run. This guard is the whole trick: a run is only ever walked from its smallest member, so across the entire algorithm each element is visited by exactly one inner walk → O(n) total, not O(n²).

frames.caption: 201 is not in the set → this run ends. Its length is 1.
frames.note: The while test failed, so we have walked the run to its top. The run we just measured is the maximal block of consecutive integers containing this start.

frames.caption: longest = max(longest, 1) = 1.
frames.note: Record this run's length against the best seen so far. longest is the running answer; only a run longer than everything before it moves the needle.

frames.caption: x=1: is 0 in the set? No → 1 is a run START. Begin counting (length = 1).
frames.note: x is a run START because x−1 is NOT in the set — nothing precedes it, so this is the smallest element of its run. This guard is the whole trick: a run is only ever walked from its smallest member, so across the entire algorithm each element is visited by exactly one inner walk → O(n) total, not O(n²).

frames.caption: 2 is in the set → extend the run. length = 2.
frames.note: From a confirmed start we extend upward: while next = x+1, x+2, … is in the set, grow the run length. Each membership test is O(1) average thanks to the hash set. We never revisit these elements as starts (their predecessor is present), so this walk is charged to them exactly once.

frames.caption: 3 is in the set → extend the run. length = 3.
frames.note: From a confirmed start we extend upward: while next = x+1, x+2, … is in the set, grow the run length. Each membership test is O(1) average thanks to the hash set. We never revisit these elements as starts (their predecessor is present), so this walk is charged to them exactly once.

frames.caption: 4 is in the set → extend the run. length = 4.
frames.note: From a confirmed start we extend upward: while next = x+1, x+2, … is in the set, grow the run length. Each membership test is O(1) average thanks to the hash set. We never revisit these elements as starts (their predecessor is present), so this walk is charged to them exactly once.

frames.caption: 5 is not in the set → this run ends. Its length is 4.
frames.note: The while test failed, so we have walked the run to its top. The run we just measured is the maximal block of consecutive integers containing this start.

frames.caption: longest = max(longest, 4) = 4.
frames.note: Record this run's length against the best seen so far. longest is the running answer; only a run longer than everything before it moves the needle.

frames.caption: x=3: is 2 in the set? Yes → 3 is mid-run, skip it.
frames.note: x−1 IS in the set, so x sits in the MIDDLE of some run and will be counted when we reach that run's smallest element. Skipping here is what prevents re-walking the same run from every member — dropping this guard is the classic mistake that makes the algorithm O(n²).

frames.caption: x=2: is 1 in the set? Yes → 2 is mid-run, skip it.
frames.note: x−1 IS in the set, so x sits in the MIDDLE of some run and will be counted when we reach that run's smallest element. Skipping here is what prevents re-walking the same run from every member — dropping this guard is the classic mistake that makes the algorithm O(n²).

frames.caption: Every value examined. The longest consecutive run has length 4 (it was {1, 2, 3, 4}).
frames.note: Each element was the subject of at most one inner walk (only run-starts walk, and they walk distinct elements), so the total inner work is O(n) despite the nested-looking loops — the O(n) time, O(n) space result.
